input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

specialist_capabilities: High-memory workstation: configured with 128 GB RAM and a 4096 GB SSD for large datasets, many VMs, or heavy multitasking
Dedicated GPU for accelerated compute and gaming: NVIDIA RTX 4060 handles GPU-accelerated creative apps and modern games at laptop class performance
Strong CPU performance: AMD Ryzen 7 7840HS (8 cores, up to 5.1 GHz) for single- and multi-threaded workloads
14.0" 2560×1600 display provides high pixel density for detailed work and UI space on a compact panel
Desktop-replacement portability: at 5.45 lbs (~2.47 kg) this is heavier than ultraportables — better for occasional transport than daily airline carry
Windows 11 Pro out of the box for business features, domain join, and enterprise management
Moderate battery endurance: up to 8 hours — sufficient for a workday with mixed productivity but shorter for sustained high-load GPU/CPU use

specialist_audience.students: Power or grad students working with large datasets or virtual machines could use it, but weight/price make it less suited for everyday classroom carry
specialist_audience.professionals: Fits professionals who need Windows Pro, large memory, and local storage for heavy applications and datasets
specialist_audience.developers: Excellent for developers running many containers, local VMs, or large builds thanks to 128 GB RAM and fast storage
specialist_audience.gamers: Good for gamers who want a powerful dedicated GPU in a compact 14" chassis, though thermals/clock targets depend on the OEM tuning
specialist_audience.creators: Well suited to creators who use GPU-accelerated editing/3D tools and need large RAM and local storage
specialist_audience.casual_home: Overkill for basic web/email/media use; most casual users won't need this level of RAM or storage
specialist_audience.power_users: Strong fit for power users who run concurrent heavy workloads, local servers, or large datasets

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the GPU VRAM amount and memory configuration for the NVIDIA RTX 4060 in this model?

What is the TDP/configuration and sustained turbo behavior for the Ryzen 7 7840HS under sustained load (expected thermal/throttling characteristics)?
What is the weight and dimensions listed for the variant I will receive (site shows 14" but model name includes '16' — please confirm)?
What is the battery capacity (Wh) and is the quoted 'up to 8 hours' measured under what workload?
What is the included warranty period and options for extended warranty or accidental damage protection?
What is the size/wattage of the supplied power adapter, and does it support fast charging?
Does the laptop include a webcam with IR or privacy shutter, and what is the microphone/ speaker configuration (quality/noise cancellation)?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.description: The Dell G15 delivers Intel Core i9-13900H performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 24GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 3456x2234 display. Up to 20 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

raw_attributes.description: The Dell G15 delivers Intel Core i9-13900H performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 24GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 3456x2234 display. Up to 20 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You classify e-commerce products by product type. Return JSON with keys:
  product_type     short noun (e.g. 'laptop', 'blender', 'headphones', 'smart-bulb', 'office-chair')
  taxonomy_path    list of broader categories from generic to specific
                   (e.g. ['electronics','computers','laptop'])
  confidence       0.0–1.0 — your certainty
If the input is too sparse to classify, return product_type='unknown' and confidence below 0.3. Never invent specs — only label.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

{
  "specialist_capabilities": [
    "Excellent for web/office productivity and multitasking with Apple M3 and 64GB RAM",
    "Good fit for creative work (photo editing, layout, light video editing) thanks to high-resolution 13.6\" 3024×1964 display and ample RAM",
    "Mainstream portability (~1.91 kg / 4.21 lb) with up to ~15 hours battery life — suitable for travel and remote work",
    "512GB SSD provides fast local storage for apps and active projects",
    "macOS Sonoma environment and Apple M3 optimize performance and power efficiency for supported apps",
    "Integrated GPU limits high-end GPU rendering, large-model ML training, and top-tier gaming performance compared with discrete-GPU laptops"
  ],
  "specialist_use_case_fit": {
    "web_dev": 0.90,
    "business": 0.90,
    "school": 0.90,
    "travel": 0.85,
    "creative": 0.80,
    "general": 0.95,
    "machine_learning": 0.35,
    "gaming": 0.20
  },
  "specialist_audience": {
    "students": "Portable, long battery life and strong multitasking memory for study and coursework",
    "professionals": "Stable macOS platform with high RAM for multitasking, office apps, and remote work",
    "developers": "Apple M3 performance and large RAM capacity support local development and running multiple tools/VMs",
    "creators": "High-resolution display and 64GB RAM suit photo editing and light-to-moderate video workflows",
    "casual_home": "Fast, responsive everyday computing with good battery life",
    "power_users": "Large 64GB RAM helps heavy multitasking and large datasets, but integrated GPU constrains very heavy GPU workloads"
  },
  "specialist_buyer_questions": [
    "Is the 64GB RAM configurable/upgradeable after purchase or fixed to this configuration?",
    "What physical ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB-C count, HDMI, SD card)?",
    "What is the display panel type (IPS/mini-LED/OLED) and color gamut (sRGB/P3) coverage?",
    "What is the display refresh rate?",
    "How does the machine thermally behave under sustained heavy CPU or video-export loads (throttling/noise)?",
    "What real-world battery life can I expect under web browsing, video playback, and heavy workloads?",
    "Does the integrated GPU accelerate the apps I plan to use (e.g., Final Cut Pro, Adobe apps, VS Code with containers)?",
    "Is external display support and resolution/refresh limits documented for this model?",
    "Is the storage user-replaceable or expandable via external/Cloud options?",
    "What warranty and AppleCare options are available, and are there region-specific service considerations?",
    "Will software I rely on (specific dev tools, virtualization, or older apps) run natively or require Rosetta/emulation on M3?"
  ]
}

input.title: Apple MacBook Air 125 13.6" 64GB RAM 512GB SSD
input.description: The Apple MacBook Air delivers Apple M3 performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 64GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 13.6-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

raw_attributes.description: The Apple MacBook Air delivers Apple M3 performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 64GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 13.6-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

specialist_capabilities: Excellent for web/office productivity and multitasking with Apple M3 and 64GB RAM
Good fit for creative work (photo editing, layout, light video editing) thanks to high-resolution 13.6" 3024×1964 display and ample RAM
Mainstream portability (~1.91 kg / 4.21 lb) with up to ~15 hours battery life — suitable for travel and remote work
512GB SSD provides fast local storage for apps and active projects
macOS Sonoma environment and Apple M3 optimize performance and power efficiency for supported apps
Integrated GPU limits high-end GPU rendering, large-model ML training, and top-tier gaming performance compared with discrete-GPU laptops

specialist_audience.students: Portable, long battery life and strong multitasking memory for study and coursework
specialist_audience.professionals: Stable macOS platform with high RAM for multitasking, office apps, and remote work
specialist_audience.developers: Apple M3 performance and large RAM capacity support local development and running multiple tools/VMs
specialist_audience.creators: High-resolution display and 64GB RAM suit photo editing and light-to-moderate video workflows
specialist_audience.casual_home: Fast, responsive everyday computing with good battery life
specialist_audience.power_users: Large 64GB RAM helps heavy multitasking and large datasets, but integrated GPU constrains very heavy GPU workloads

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the 64GB RAM configurable/upgradeable after purchase or fixed to this configuration?
What physical ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB-C count, HDMI, SD card)?
What is the display panel type (IPS/mini-LED/OLED) and color gamut (sRGB/P3) coverage?
What is the display refresh rate?
How does the machine thermally behave under sustained heavy CPU or video-export loads (throttling/noise)?
What real-world battery life can I expect under web browsing, video playback, and heavy workloads?
Does the integrated GPU accelerate the apps I plan to use (e.g., Final Cut Pro, Adobe apps, VS Code with containers)?
Is external display support and resolution/refresh limits documented for this model?
Is the storage user-replaceable or expandable via external/Cloud options?
What warranty and AppleCare options are available, and are there region-specific service considerations?
Will software I rely on (specific dev tools, virtualization, or older apps) run natively or require Rosetta/emulation on M3?

Tag key rules:

  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.title: Acer Nitro 196 17.3" 16GB RAM 512GB SSD
input.description: The Acer Nitro delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 3456x2234 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

raw_attributes.description: The Acer Nitro delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 3456x2234 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

specialist_capabilities: 17.3" high-resolution display (3456×2234) suitable for detailed document/layout work and multitasking on a large canvas
Battery life rated up to 15 hours — good for long unplugged sessions relative to a 17.3" laptop
AMD Ryzen 5 7640U (6 cores, up to 4.9 GHz) offers solid multi‑threaded performance for office, browsing and light content tasks
16 GB RAM and 512 GB SSD provide a capable baseline for web development, office productivity and general multitasking
Integrated Intel Iris Xe GPU enables light/older or low‑detail gaming and GPU‑accelerated media tasks, but is not a discrete GPU for demanding gaming or GPU compute
Desktop‑replacement weight class (~5.3 lb / ~2.4 kg) — larger screen with lower portability compared with ultraportables
Windows 11 Home preinstalled — broad software compatibility for Windows applications and developer tools
Key upgrade/unknowns flagged: RAM/config upgradeability, panel type (IPS/OLED) and I/O capabilities (Thunderbolt/USB‑C, HDMI, card reader) are not specified in the listing

specialist_audience.students: Large display and long battery are good for study and media, but the 17.3" size and ~5.3 lb weight reduce daily portability.
specialist_audience.professionals: Strong CPU and long battery suit productivity and multitasking; good as a desktop‑replacement office machine.
specialist_audience.developers: 16 GB RAM, a 6‑core Ryzen CPU and big screen support dev workflows and local testing, though heavier than ultraportables.
specialist_audience.gamers: Only for light or older titles — integrated Iris Xe can handle casual gaming but not demanding modern AAA games at high settings.
specialist_audience.creators: High resolution and a large canvas help photo/video editing and layout work, but panel color‑specs and discrete GPU are not specified so not ideal for pro color‑critical or GPU‑accelerated workflows.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Excellent for media, browsing and general home use thanks to the big high‑res display and solid battery life.
specialist_audience.power_users: May be limiting for heavy GPU compute or high‑end content creation due to lack of a discrete GPU and only 16 GB RAM.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the panel type (IPS, OLED, VA) and what are its color‑space coverage numbers (sRGB/Adobe RGB/DCI‑P3)?
Is the display matte or glossy, and what is the measured brightness (nits)?
Does this model include a discrete GPU option, or is it limited to Intel Iris Xe integrated graphics?
Is the 512 GB drive NVMe PCIe and user‑replaceable/upgradeable?
Is the RAM soldered or does the laptop have an SO‑DIMM slot for future RAM upgrades?
What ports are available (USB‑C, Thunderbolt, HDMI, Ethernet, SD card reader)?
What is the charger wattage and how long does the battery life rating assume (real‑world vs. manufacturer test)?
What are the laptop's exact dimensions (thickness) and weight in kilograms?
How is the thermal performance under sustained load (CPU/GPU throttling, fan noise levels)?
What webcam resolution/mic quality is included and is there a privacy shutter?
Which Wi‑Fi and Bluetooth versions are supported (Wi‑Fi 6/6E, Bluetooth 5.x)?
Is Windows 11 Home the only OS option, or is Windows 11 Pro available? Are there drivers for Linux if needed?
What warranty length and support options come with the laptop?
Are there configurations with more RAM, larger SSDs, or different CPUs in this model line?

  good_for_tags    object {tag_key: confidence 0.0-1.0}
Tag key rules:
  - snake_case prefixed with 'good_for_' (e.g. good_for_gaming, good_for_baby_food).
  - Tag vocabulary is open: pick whatever the product genuinely supports.
  - Only emit tags backed by evidence in the input. Lower confidence = weaker evidence; omit tags below 0.2.
  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

You classify e-commerce products by product type. Return JSON with keys:
  product_type     short noun (e.g. 'laptop', 'blender', 'headphones', 'smart-bulb', 'office-chair')
  taxonomy_path    list of broader categories from generic to specific
                   (e.g. ['electronics','computers','laptop'])
  confidence       0.0–1.0 — your certainty
If the input is too sparse to classify, return product_type='unknown' and confidence below 0.3. Never invent specs — only label.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.description: The Lenovo Legion delivers Intel Core i7-1360P performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 15.6-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 10 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

raw_attributes.description: The Lenovo Legion delivers Intel Core i7-1360P performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 15.6-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 10 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

specialist_capabilities: Mid-range gaming capable — NVIDIA RTX 4050 mobile GPU supports smooth 1080p and reasonable 1440p gaming at medium-to-high settings; native 2880×1800 will be more demanding
Ultraportable class (~2.79 lbs / ~1.27 kg) with up to 10 hours battery life — suited for travel and all-day mobile use
Strong multitasking and productivity CPU performance — 12-core Intel Core i7-1360P for compilation, office work, and multitasking
Good for GPU-accelerated creative tasks (rendering, hardware encoding) — GPU present, though exact VRAM and panel color accuracy are unspecified
16 GB RAM and 256 GB SSD adequate for general productivity, web development, and light content projects but may constrain large datasets or heavy multitasking
Not ideal for heavy machine-learning training — RAM and unspecified VRAM likely limit large-model training; suitable for small experiments/inference
Windows 11 Home — wide software and game compatibility out of the box
High-resolution 15.6" 2880×1800 display gives high pixel density for sharper text and images; panel type, brightness and color gamut not provided
Upgradeability not specified — buyers should confirm RAM/SSD expansion options before purchase if they need 32 GB+ RAM or large storage

specialist_audience.students: Lightweight, long battery life and capable CPU for coursework and remote classes
specialist_audience.professionals: Strong CPU and discrete GPU for productivity, presentations, and business apps
specialist_audience.developers: 12-core i7 and fast storage help builds and local testing; portable for hybrid work
specialist_audience.gamers: RTX 4050 enables mid-range gaming on a portable 15.6" chassis
specialist_audience.creators: GPU acceleration and high-res screen help photo/video editing; verify panel color accuracy for color-critical work
specialist_audience.casual_home: Well suited for streaming, browsing, and everyday media consumption
specialist_audience.power_users: Capable CPU/GPU but 16 GB RAM and 256 GB SSD may be limiting for large projects — confirm upgrade options

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the display panel type (IPS, OLED, etc.) and its color coverage (sRGB / Adobe RGB / DCI‑P3)?
What is the screen refresh rate (60Hz, 120Hz, 144Hz, etc.) and maximum brightness (nits)?
How much dedicated VRAM does the NVIDIA RTX 4050 have?
Is the 16 GB of RAM soldered or user-upgradeable? Are there free SO‑DIMM slots and is 32 GB supported?
Is the 256 GB SSD NVMe (PCIe) and is there an additional M.2 slot or room for a 2.5" drive for expansion?
Which external ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB-C with PD, HDMI version, USB-A, Ethernet, SD card reader)?
What is the sustained thermal performance — does the CPU/GPU throttle under long gaming or rendering sessions?
What is the battery capacity in Wh and what charging options/wattage does the charger support? Is USB-C PD supported?
What are the laptop's exact dimensions and confirmed weight in both lbs and kg?
Which Wi‑Fi and Bluetooth standards are supported (Wi‑Fi 6/6E, Bluetooth 5.x)?
What is the quality of the webcam and microphones, and does the keyboard have per-key backlight or a numeric keypad?
What warranty, on-site service, or extended support options come with the unit?

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Lenovo ThinkPad X1 161 17.3" 32GB RAM 4096GB SSD
input.description: The Lenovo ThinkPad X1 delivers Intel Core i7-1360P performance with NVIDIA RTX 4080 graphics. 32GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 18 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

raw_attributes.description: The Lenovo ThinkPad X1 delivers Intel Core i7-1360P performance with NVIDIA RTX 4080 graphics. 32GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 18 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

specialist_capabilities: Dedicated NVIDIA RTX 4080 GPU — suited to high-end GPU-accelerated workloads such as gaming, realtime rendering, and GPU inference (verify mobile variant/VRAM for large ML training jobs).
Intel Core i7-1360P (12 cores, up to 5.0 GHz) — strong single- and multi-threaded CPU performance for multitasking, builds and productivity tasks.
32 GB RAM and 4,096 GB SSD — large memory and very high local storage capacity for large projects, VMs, datasets and media libraries.
17.3" 2880×1800 display — high-resolution large workspace good for editing, CAD, video timelines and multi-pane development.
Relatively lightweight for a 17.3" class laptop (3.72 lb / ~1.69 kg) — better portablity than typical desktop-replacement 17.3" systems.
Up to 18 hours battery life (manufacturer claim) — long battery endurance for travel or mobile workflows (actual runtime will vary by workload).
Windows 11 Home — broad application compatibility out of the box (business users may prefer Windows Pro for domain/enterprise features).

specialist_audience.students: Powerful hardware may be overkill for basic coursework; suitable for engineering/media students who need GPU and large storage but consider size/price.
specialist_audience.professionals: Large screen, strong CPU/GPU and long battery life make it a good fit for mobile professionals who run demanding apps.
specialist_audience.developers: 32 GB RAM, many cores and large SSD support heavy local builds, containers and VMs.
specialist_audience.gamers: High-end RTX GPU and large display target gamers, though frame rates depend on display refresh rate and cooling implementation.
specialist_audience.creators: High-res 17.3" panel, RTX GPU and 4 TB storage suit video editing, 3D and content-creation workflows.
specialist_audience.casual_home: More performance and capacity than most casual users need; ideal if you want a future-proof, powerful machine.
specialist_audience.power_users: Strong specs (CPU cores, RTX GPU, large RAM and SSD) meet the needs of demanding multi-app, media and compute users.

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the RTX 4080 the mobile (laptop) variant and how much VRAM does it have?
What is the display panel type (IPS, OLED) and what color gamut / color accuracy does it offer (sRGB/AdobeRGB/P3)?
What is the display refresh rate (60Hz, 120Hz, 165Hz, etc.)?
What ports are included (Thunderbolt 4 / USB‑C PD, HDMI, Ethernet, SD card reader) and what bandwidth do they support?
Is the RAM soldered or user-upgradeable and are there additional RAM slots?
Is the 4,096 GB SSD a single NVMe drive or multiple drives, and is the storage user-upgradeable/replaceable?
What are real-world battery runtimes under mixed/web browsing, video playback and heavy GPU load?
How is the thermal performance and sustained boost behavior under long gaming or rendering sessions?
What is the size and weight in metric units and the physical dimensions (to confirm travel/fit in bags)?
Does it include enterprise features common to ThinkPad line (TPM, fingerprint reader, IR webcam) or require a Windows Pro upgrade?
What is the warranty period and what on-site or accidental damage coverage options are available?
What is the charger wattage and can the laptop charge via USB-C PD for travel convenience?
Are there driver/firmware differences for Linux support if I plan to run Linux?
What is the exact model number/part number for confirming specs and support information?
Is there information on display hinge, keyboard quality (backlight, travel), and webcam/audio quality for conferencing?

input.system:
You extract product specifications from unstructured text. Return JSON with keys:
  parsed_specs        flat object of normalized specs you found (e.g. {'ram_gb': 16, 'wattage_w': 1200, 'capacity_l': 1.5})
  parsed_source_fields object mapping each spec key to the source field you read it from ('title' | 'description' | 'raw_attributes')
Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).
  - Numeric values must be numbers, not strings.
  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.description: The Lenovo Yoga delivers Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 32GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 13.3-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 9 hours of battery life. Ships in Midnight.

composer_decisions.reason: taxonomy classifier result has precedence for product_type
structured specialist fit scores may be included

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

If the input is too sparse to classify, return product_type='unknown' and confidence below 0.3. Never invent specs — only label.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.
