You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

specialist_audience.professionals: Professionals needing a portable-ish workstation for CPU/GPU tasks and large local storage.

specialist_audience.casual_home: Overkill for casual browsing or streaming; specs target power users, not basic home use.
specialist_audience.power_users: Designed for users who need extreme multitasking, large local datasets, or workstation capabilities in a portable chassis.

What ports are available (Thunderbolt/USB4, USB-A, HDMI, SD card reader, Ethernet via adapter) and how many external displays are supported?

What panel type and color coverage does the 3840×2400 display have (IPS/P-Touch, OLED, measured sRGB/Adobe RGB/DCI-P3 %)?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Apple MacBook Air 149 13.0" 96GB RAM 4096GB SSD
input.description: The Apple MacBook Air delivers Apple M2 performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 96GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 13.0-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 7 hours of battery life. Ships in Space Gray.

raw_attributes.description: The Apple MacBook Air delivers Apple M2 performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 96GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 13.0-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 7 hours of battery life. Ships in Space Gray.

specialist_capabilities: Very large memory: 96 GB RAM — supports heavy multitasking and large in-memory datasets
Massive fast storage: 4096 GB SSD — ample local space for large projects and media libraries
Apple M2 SoC with integrated Apple GPU — power-efficient CPU/GPU for everyday to moderately heavy workloads
13.0" 2560×1600 high-resolution display — good pixel density for text and photo work on a compact panel
Ultra-portable class weight (2.49 lbs / ~1.13 kg) — easy to carry for travel or commuting
macOS Sonoma — integration with Apple ecosystem and macOS-only apps
Battery rated up to 7 hours — moderate endurance (may be less under sustained heavy load)
No discrete GPU — limited for high-end gaming and GPU-heavy rendering/training compared with systems that have dedicated GPUs

specialist_audience.students: Lightweight, high-res 13" display and large storage make it good for coursework and media; battery is moderate.
specialist_audience.professionals: Large RAM and 4 TB SSD suit heavy multitasking, large local datasets, and business workflows within macOS.
specialist_audience.developers: Fast M2 CPU, large RAM and storage support local builds, VMs/containers and large codebases; macOS-native toolchain.
specialist_audience.creators: High RAM and large SSD help photo/video projects, but integrated GPU limits heavy GPU render/export performance versus discrete-GPU machines.
specialist_audience.gamers: Can run many macOS titles at modest settings, but not ideal for high-frame-rate AAA gaming due to integrated GPU and platform limitations.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Excellent for web, media playback and everyday tasks; roomy storage for movies and photos.
specialist_audience.power_users: Exceptional memory and storage capacity for memory-heavy workflows, but verify ports and upgradeability for docking/expansion needs.

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the listed 96 GB RAM soldered/unified and non-user-upgradeable after purchase?
Is the 4096 GB SSD removable or upgradable, and is it Apple-specified/configured storage?
What exact Apple M2 variant is included (regular M2, M2 Pro, etc.) and what are the GPU core counts?
How many and what type of external ports are provided (Thunderbolt/USB-C, HDMI, SD card)?
What is the display panel type (IPS / OLED / Liquid Retina) and peak brightness (nits) / color gamut coverage (sRGB/AdobeRGB/P3)?
Is the listed 7 hours battery life measured under light usage or mixed usage? What are expected real-world runtimes for video playback and mixed workloads?
Does this MacBook support two or more external displays, and at what maximum resolution/refresh rates?
Is there an included charger and what is its wattage? Does it support fast charging?
What warranty and AppleCare options are available with this unit?
Is this a new sealed unit from Apple or a custom/configured/third-party-modified configuration?
Are there any factory or seller-provided benchmarks or stress-test results for sustained CPU/GPU workloads and thermal performance?
Does this model include Touch ID, the current Magic Keyboard layout, and an upgraded webcam/mic array?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

specialist_capabilities: High-performance 16-core Intel Core Ultra 7 155H for CPU-bound multitasking and heavy single‑thread bursts (up to 4.8 GHz).
Integrated Intel Iris Xe graphics — fine for web, office, video playback and light photo editing; not a substitute for a discrete GPU for 3D rendering or high‑end gaming.
24 GB RAM — comfortable for large browser sessions, many concurrent apps and medium‑sized creative projects; below typical thresholds for large ML training workloads.
8192 GB SSD (8,192 GB) — very large local NVMe storage capacity for extensive media libraries, projects, or local VMs (speed/generation not specified).
16.0" 2880×1800 high‑resolution display — high pixel density suitable for detailed work; panel type and color gamut are not specified.
Ultraportable weight class (~2.66 lbs / ~1.2 kg) — well suited to frequent travel and working on the go.
Windows 11 Home — broad application compatibility for business, education and general productivity.
Battery life rated up to ~6 hours — moderate; expect shorter runtime under heavy CPU or display load.

specialist_audience.students: Very portable, plenty of RAM and huge storage for projects and media; battery life is moderate so daily charging may be needed.
specialist_audience.professionals: Strong CPU performance and light weight make it good for business travel and multitasking; Windows 11 Home ensures wide app compatibility.
specialist_audience.developers: High core count and 24 GB RAM support local builds, containers and VMs for many dev workflows; lack of discrete GPU limits GPU-accelerated tasks.
specialist_audience.gamers: Integrated Iris Xe only — can handle casual/indie titles but not modern AAA gaming at high settings.
specialist_audience.creators: High-res 16" screen and large SSD are useful for photo/video libraries; unknown panel color accuracy and lack of discrete GPU constrain professional color-critical or GPU‑accelerated workflows.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Excellent: light, fast CPU, lots of storage for media, and Windows app ecosystem.
specialist_audience.power_users: Strong CPU and very large storage help many demanding workflows, but absence of a discrete GPU and RAM under 32 GB limit some specialist power‑user scenarios (e.g., large ML training, heavy GPU rendering).

You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

{
  "specialist_capabilities": [
    "High multitasking headroom: Intel Core i7-1360P plus 48 GB RAM for large working sets, heavy multitasking and multiple VMs",

    "Long battery life rated up to 17 hours for all-day unplugged use",
    "Mainstream portable weight class (~3.4 lb / 1.54 kg) — portable but not ultra-light",

  ],

    "gamers": "OK for casual and esports titles at modest settings; not optimized for AAA gaming",
    "creators": "Capable for photo editing and light video work, but limited by integrated GPU and 256 GB storage",
    "casual_home": "Great all-around daily driver for web, media and office tasks",

  },
  "specialist_buyer_questions": [

    "What is the display panel type and color accuracy (sRGB/AdobeRGB coverage, factory calibration)?",

    "What sustained CPU/GPU performance and thermals can be expected under long workloads (throttling/fan noise)?",
    "What is the battery capacity (Wh) and under what test conditions is the 17-hour rating measured?",

    "Is there support for Linux or documented Linux compatibility if I prefer a non-Windows OS?",

    "Are there configuration options with larger SSD capacity or a discrete GPU, or recommended upgrade paths?"
  ]
}

  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

specialist_audience.creators: High-res display, 32 GB RAM and large SSD suit photo/video editing and 3D/rendering workflows; color gamut/accuracy not specified.
specialist_audience.casual_home: More performance and cost than most casual users need, but excellent for long battery life and media.
specialist_audience.power_users: Excellent: large RAM and huge SSD support heavy multitasking, local data sets, and demanding applications.

  good_for_tags    object {tag_key: confidence 0.0-1.0}
Tag key rules:
  - snake_case prefixed with 'good_for_' (e.g. good_for_gaming, good_for_baby_food).
  - Tag vocabulary is open: pick whatever the product genuinely supports.
  - Only emit tags backed by evidence in the input. Lower confidence = weaker evidence; omit tags below 0.2.
  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).

  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)

Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.description: The Dell Inspiron delivers Intel Core i9-13900H performance with NVIDIA RTX 4090 graphics. 128GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 18.0-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 13 hours of battery life. Ships in Space Gray.

raw_attributes.description: The Dell Inspiron delivers Intel Core i9-13900H performance with NVIDIA RTX 4090 graphics. 128GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 18.0-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 13 hours of battery life. Ships in Space Gray.

specialist_capabilities: High-end CPU: Intel Core i9-13900H for sustained multi-threaded and single-threaded performance.
Top-tier GPU: NVIDIA RTX 4090 for GPU-accelerated rendering, real-time ray tracing, and high-frame-rate gaming workloads.
Very large system memory: 128 GB RAM suitable for large in-memory datasets, multiple VMs, and heavy multitasking.
High-resolution large display: 18.0" 3840×2400 panel provides very high pixel density for detailed creative work and desktop-like workspace.
Portable for its class: 3.91 lb (~1.77 kg) places this in mainstream portable territory for an 18" machine.
Decent stated battery endurance: up to 13 hours (manufacturer figure) for light-to-moderate use.
Fast primary storage: 512 GB SSD for quick OS/app responsiveness (capacity may be limiting for large projects).
Windows 11 Home preinstalled — broad software compatibility on Windows ecosystem.

specialist_audience.students: Capable for advanced study workloads but likely overpowered and expensive for typical student needs.
specialist_audience.professionals: Good fit for professionals who need high CPU/GPU performance and large RAM capacity.
specialist_audience.developers: Strong choice for developers working with large builds, local VMs, or GPU-accelerated tooling.
specialist_audience.gamers: High-end GPU and CPU suit demanding gaming; large 18" display benefits immersive play.
specialist_audience.creators: High resolution screen, strong GPU and abundant RAM support photo/video editing, 3D work, and rendering.
specialist_audience.casual_home: More power than typical casual users need; storage capacity may be limiting for large media libraries.
specialist_audience.power_users: Designed for power users needing maximum RAM and GPU performance in a portable chassis.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the laptop GPU's VRAM capacity and is it the full-power RTX 4090 mobile variant or a trimmed TGP configuration?

  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You generate soft 'good_for_*' tags for an e-commerce product. Return JSON with one key:
  good_for_tags    object {tag_key: confidence 0.0-1.0}
Tag key rules:
  - snake_case prefixed with 'good_for_' (e.g. good_for_gaming, good_for_baby_food).
  - Tag vocabulary is open: pick whatever the product genuinely supports.
  - Only emit tags backed by evidence in the input. Lower confidence = weaker evidence; omit tags below 0.2.
  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Dell XPS 074 13.6" 32GB RAM 4096GB SSD
input.description: The Dell XPS delivers Intel Core Ultra 7 155H performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 32GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 13.6-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 19 hours of battery life. Ships in Midnight.

raw_attributes.description: The Dell XPS delivers Intel Core Ultra 7 155H performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 32GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 13.6-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 19 hours of battery life. Ships in Midnight.

specialist_capabilities: High CPU throughput: Intel Core Ultra 7 155H with 16 cores and up to 4.8 GHz — strong for heavy multitasking and CPU-bound workloads.
Generous memory & local storage: 32 GB RAM and 4096 GB (4 TB) SSD — suitable for large projects, many browser tabs/VMs, and large local media libraries.
High-resolution 13.6" display (3024 × 1964) — high pixel density for detailed image work and sharp text.
Integrated AMD Radeon 780M graphics — accelerates light/medium gaming and GPU‑accelerated creative tasks but is not a high-end discrete GPU for sustained 3D rendering or large ML training jobs.
Long manufacturer-rated battery life (up to 19 hours) and mainstream portability (~3.8 lb / 1.72 kg) — balanced for full-day mobile use without being an ultra‑light ultraportable.
Windows 11 Home — broad compatibility with business, developer, and consumer apps.
Listing lacks detailed I/O and serviceability info — confirm Thunderbolt/USB4, HDMI, SD card, and whether RAM/SSD are user-replaceable before buying.

specialist_audience.students: All-day battery, high-res screen and ample RAM/storage make it good for coursework, research, and media.
specialist_audience.professionals: Strong CPU, lots of RAM and storage, and long battery life suit productivity and multitasking.
specialist_audience.developers: Plenty of RAM and CPU cores for local builds, containers, and IDEs; Windows compatibility for common toolchains.
specialist_audience.gamers: Capable for casual and some modern titles at modest settings thanks to Radeon 780M, but not a desktop-class gaming rig.
specialist_audience.creators: High-res screen and 32 GB RAM are helpful for photo/video editing and content creation on the go; heavy GPU renders will be slower than with discrete GPUs.
specialist_audience.casual_home: More power and storage than typical home laptops—great for media, multitasking, and future‑proofing.
specialist_audience.power_users: Large RAM and 4 TB SSD meet power-user needs for local datasets, VMs, and demanding multitasking, though GPU-limited for heavy 3D/ML workloads.

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the Radeon 780M integrated or a discrete/chiplet implementation on this model?
What panel type is used (IPS, OLED, etc.) and what are the display color specs (sRGB/AdobeRGB/P3 coverage, Delta E)?
Which ports are present? (Thunderbolt/USB4, USB-A, HDMI, headphone jack, SD card reader)
Is the SSD user-replaceable/upgradeable? How many M.2 slots are available?
Is the RAM soldered or upgradeable?
What is the rated battery capacity (Wh) and under what conditions was the 'up to 19 hours' measured?
What is the included charger wattage and does it support USB-C charging?
How does the system perform thermally under sustained CPU/GPU load (throttling, fan noise)?
Does the laptop include Windows 11 Home license only, and are there Windows Pro upgrade options?
What warranty and on-site support options are included or available?
Is there an IR camera or Windows Hello biometric sign-in?
What is the webcam resolution and microphone quality for video calls?
Are there user reviews or benchmarks for creative apps (Photoshop, Premiere) and gaming performance?
Is the laptop validated for Linux (drivers, suspend/resume) if I plan to install another OS?
Does the chassis/finish (Midnight) scratch or show fingerprints easily, and are case/skin accessories recommended?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

  - Tag vocabulary is open: pick whatever the product genuinely supports.
  - Only emit tags backed by evidence in the input. Lower confidence = weaker evidence; omit tags below 0.2.
  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: ASUS ZenBook 186 13.3" 16GB RAM 2048GB SSD
input.description: The ASUS ZenBook delivers AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS performance with NVIDIA RTX 4060 graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 13.3-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 17 hours of battery life. Ships in Space Gray.

raw_attributes.description: The ASUS ZenBook delivers AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS performance with NVIDIA RTX 4060 graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 13.3-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 17 hours of battery life. Ships in Space Gray.

specialist_capabilities: High single- and multi-thread CPU performance from AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS (8 cores, up to 5.2 GHz) — good for compute-heavy apps and multitasking
Dedicated NVIDIA RTX 4060 GPU for smooth 1080p/1440p gaming and GPU-accelerated creative workloads
2 TB NVMe SSD provides ample, fast local storage for large projects and games
13.3" 2560×1600 panel delivers high pixel density for detailed text and images (good for productivity and photo editing)
Long rated battery life (up to 17 hours) suited to long workdays or travel away from outlets
16 GB RAM — solid for multitasking, development, and light-to-moderate creative work, but can be limiting for large datasets or heavy video projects
Windows 11 Home out of the box — ready for Windows-native apps and drivers

specialist_audience.students: Long battery and strong CPU/GPU make it capable for coursework and light creative tasks, though the unit is relatively heavy for a 13.3" laptop.
specialist_audience.professionals: Fast CPU, dedicated GPU and large SSD suit power users who need performance on the go and long battery life.
specialist_audience.developers: Fast storage and CPU speed help builds and VMs; 16 GB RAM is fine for many dev workflows but may be limiting for heavy container/VM loads.
specialist_audience.gamers: Portable, powerful GPU for 1080p/1440p gaming—good for on-the-go sessions though thermal headroom may be more limited in a compact chassis.
specialist_audience.creators: High-res display and RTX assist GPU-accelerated editing; 16 GB RAM and unknown color gamut make it better for photo and light video than large-scale production.
specialist_audience.casual_home: More performance than most casual users need, but excellent snappy responsiveness and large storage for media.
specialist_audience.power_users: Strong baseline specs (Ryzen 9, RTX 4060, 2 TB SSD) but fixed 16 GB RAM and unknown upgradeability may be a constraint for extreme workloads.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the display panel type (IPS, OLED, etc.) and what are the measured color gamuts (sRGB / AdobeRGB / DCI-P3)?
What is the screen's refresh rate (60 Hz, 120 Hz, etc.)?
How much VRAM does the laptop's RTX 4060 have?
Is the 16 GB of RAM soldered or upgradeable? If upgradable, what is the maximum supported RAM configuration?
Are there additional M.2 slots or is the 2 TB SSD the only storage option?
What ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB‑C with PD, HDMI, USB‑A, SD card reader)?
What is the real-world battery life under typical workloads (web browsing, video playback, gaming)?
How does the chassis handle thermals and sustained CPU/GPU performance — is there thermal throttling under load?
What is the laptop's actual weight in kilograms (given 5.69 lb in the listing) and is that typical for this model?
What charger wattage is included and does it support USB‑C charging?
How loud are the fans under typical and heavy loads?
Is Windows 11 Home the OS shipped; is there an option/upgrade to Windows 11 Pro for business features?
What webcam, microphone, and speaker quality can be expected for video calls?
Is there an active warranty and what does it cover (international coverage, on-site repairs)?
Does the system include a TPM / hardware security features and does it support BitLocker?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.
